title: Cleaning Up (free) 🗑️
description: Prevent your app from crashing the system.

slides.title: With Great Power... 🦸‍♂️
slides.content:
When you create standard variables like `int x = 5;`, C guarantees that the memory will be automatically deleted and cleaned up the moment the function finishes.

But Dynamic Memory (`malloc`) is built differently.

slides.title: The Memory Leak 🚰
slides.content:
When you use `malloc`, that memory is permanently locked under your name until you explicitly tell the OS you are done with it.

If you have a game loop running 60 times a second, and every frame you `malloc` 1 MB of memory without ever releasing it, your program will rapidly consume all the RAM on the entire computer until the OS violently force-kills your app. This is called a **Memory Leak**.

slides.title: Using free()
slides.content: To release the memory back to the Operating System, you must pass the pointer to the `free()` function when you are finished.

quizData.question: What happens if you use `free()` on a pointer, and then try to read the data from that pointer on the very next line of code?

options.text: You trigger undefined behavior (often a Segfault crash) because you gave the memory back to the OS and no longer have permission to look at it.

quizData.explanation: `free` revokes your ownership. The pointer still physically holds the address, but if you attempt to use it, the OS will punish you.

quizData.question: A massive, slowly compounding crash caused by a programmer forgetting to `free` memory requested by `malloc` is famously called a Memory ______.
quizData.explanation: Memory leaks are the number one cause of applications slowly grinding to a halt over the course of several hours of run-time.
